sentences.text: Well, we were bogged down on top of the beach.
And we stayed in foxholes on top of Omaha Beach until there was some forward movements by our troops.
It was extremely heavy fighting all around.
And there I called a [NON-ENGLISH], which anyone that knows world history of World War II knows was extremely heavy fighting, lots of casualties.
And we finally were able to advance.
We were always attached to combat unit.
This was an armored division we were a part of.
And--
I'm not sure anymore which armored division it was because there were always-- there was once a 2nd Armored and 5th Armored, but I don't know which one this one was.
It was a tank unit.
And--
When the casualties come in, we set up our tents, the surgical units.
And we immediately treated the wounded soldiers.
And--
Well, in the very beginning, I did all sorts of things-- surgical dressings.
And once, I recall, I had to-- I was in charge of the personal effects of deceased soldiers.
That was a traumatic thing.
I'd have to take their wallets and letters in their pockets, pictures they carried, and put them in little boxes and send them so that the next of kin would have them.
They were very difficult days.
We were constantly being shelled.
And the Germans had a gun called the 88, which was a powerful gun, which could-- did a lot of damage to our troops.
And eventually, we broke out of Normandy.
And then we surrounded like at least 10,000 German troops south of Normandy and east of Normandy, called in the Falaise Gap.
And there, for the first time-- I already had treated some German soldiers at the-- at the hospital.
But there, for the first time, we had masses and masses of German prisoners.
There must have been at least 10,000 we captured.
And then, it became apparent that my fluent knowledge of German came in handy.
And they used me as a prisoner guard.
And they used me as an interpreter to begin with.
And we were just extremely busy, always moving.
We were constantly onward.
The moment the unit moved down another five miles or 20 miles, we had to take our tents down and keep on going with the units.
And what we did, primarily, we treated the early wounded, and then sent them to an evacuation hospital which was behind the lines, not as close as we were.
As I said, we were shelled.
We were bombed, and you name it.
And that way, we went all across to Paris, first of all, and to, to, to Alsace-Lorraine, the French part of Alsace-Lorraine.
Yeah.
Hate.
Plain hate.
I wish I could have leveled my-- I had a 30 caliber carbine which I carried with me.
I wish I could have leveled it and just killed a bunch of them.
And I-- we couldn't do that, you know?
They were prisoners of war.
We were much more humane than others.
And no, but just plain hate.
But many of them were beaten down.
And they-- many of them realized the war was lost.
And some, you could almost pity, especially the heavily wounded ones, you know?
Yeah.
Did you speak to any of them?
Oh, yeah.
I spoke to many of them.
I always asked them where they came from, trying to find out if some of them came from the area that I had lived in before, in Germany.
I might have on one or two occasions, but there was not-- they didn't-- they couldn't care less, you know?
They were just prisoners of war, and we were the American troops.
That was not something that I mentioned.
I might have mentioned it-- I know I mentioned it one time when I guarded some prisoners.
And there was a, an SS storm trooper who had a band on his sleeve.
It called-- it said, 'Adolph Hitler Leibstandarte.' That means Adolph Hitler's personal body guard.
And these American soldiers didn't really know as much about the SS as our German army, as I already knew then.
And I singled that bastard out, and I took him away from the rest of the prisoners.
And he stood at attention.
He said, ya vol!
You know, this typical German.
And I said, here's a shovel.
Dig.
Dig.
And he said, what should I dig?
Latrines.
I had him dig latrines.
And I told him that, if he as much as moved a foot away from digging that latrine, that I would shoot him.
And the sad part was, what really bothered me even more, when I talked German to him, I found out he had an accent.
And the accent was Dutch.
And he was a Dutch man, a citizen of Holland who had volunteered, volunteered to be in the SS Leibstandarte.
I knew that because only volunteers were there.
Of course, he claimed that he was drafted.
And you know, he told me all these lies, but I knew better.
And I was really hoping that he would make a run for it.
I had taken the safety off my gun just for that purpose.
I was going to kill the son of a bitch, but he didn't run.
He kept on digging latrines.
I mean, he didn't even want to look up at me.
He knew that I had it in for him.
That's it.
I told him I was a Jew.
A German Jew, I told him.
And he kept on digging, let me tell you.
So that was a little side story.
I shouldn't tell all these stories.
It's a little side story.
In any case, we finally--
Oh, yes.
Paris, one or two days after its liberation.
Because I spoke enough French that I had my ways of getting to Paris.
We were outside of Paris, again, with an armored division.
And they wouldn't let us march into Paris.
We were the first ones there.
But they gave a French-- the free French the green light.
They said, you go in, and then we go in.
And that's what happened a couple days later.
And it was fabulous.
I mean, they threw flowers at us.
They gave us wine.
And the girls were galore.
My goodness, my goodness, what went on.
I mean, it was a wonderful experience to be in Paris in August of 1944.
That's when I was there.
And then we went-- after we had liberated Paris, then we went as far as the French part of Alsace-Lorraine where some very heavy fighting took place near a city called Luneville.
I recall that town.

And after Luneville and some surrounding places had been liberated, we then went as far as the Maginot Line, near the-- which is across from the German secret line on the border between Germany and France and until we finally crossed the Rhine.
And that was after the Battle of the Bulge.
Thanks god some of my members of my unit all had to go into that battle.
And some didn't make it, but I was saved.
I did not-- I did not have to go to the Battle of the Bulge.
It was south of it at a town near Vichy, near [NON-ENGLISH], which is all in Alsace-Lorraine.
That's where my unit was based.
And then, shortly after the Battle of the Ardennes was over and done with, we had to then pack up our unit and follow combat units into Germany.
We went-- we criss-crossed.
We went into Germany.
And then we actually went back into France.
The border was sort of a zigzag.
And some parts had been liberated, others had not.
So one day I was in Germany, and another time I went back for the second time.
I have a letter which talks of an experience in one German town that we had liberated where I was then assigned to requisition housing for all our unit that no longer wanted to live in tents.
You know, we were in tents-- either pup tents or regular tents-- all across France.
All across France, we were in these units, in these pup tents or regular tents.

And when we got to Germany and they found out I spoke German, I quickly requisitioned houses, first for the officers-- naturally, I always saw to it that I have good quarters for myself-- and for the troops.
And some-- one of these experience, I have in a letter.
And if you don't mind, I'll read excerpts.
A letter which I wrote to my wife on the 26th of March, 1945.
And I write here that 'We've been moving around quite a bit lately.
I'm now deep in Germany--' it wasn't that deep, but deep for us-- '--and quite a stretch from France's border.
It's really the second time that I re-entered this rotten country during the last few days.
But we're here to stay now, and God knows when I'll ever see France again.
You can tell by my news-- by the newscasts that we made quite a bit of progress along the Western Front during the last few weeks and crossed the Rhine in many places.' Which we did.
We got across on a pontoon bridge. 'I've been with the Army for some time now and have been through cities all through Alsace and Lorraine.
And I then traveled through the Maginot Line and the impregnable, quote, unquote, 'secret line.' Both are mightily strong, heavily fortified defense lines.
But our dough boys and tanks crashed them just the same.
Well, now I am back in Germany sitting in a little room overlooking mountains and woods and the suburbs of a beautiful German city--' which I couldn't name, but I can tell you now what it is.
It was called Naishtat on the Rhine Schlosser.
All right?
Near what large city?
That was in southern Germany somewhere.
It was a very beautiful area, OK? 'This city did not take too much of a beating by our Air Force, artillery, and so on.
And we live in a big three-story house which belonged to a German industrialist, an ardent Nazi who fled with the retreating German troops.
Several Russian slave laborers greeted us with bottles of wine upon our arrival and seem to have done an excellent job of smashing everything in their former boss's house.
They just ruined it inside.' They had cut the oriental carpetings.
They had cut the, the paintings on the wall.
They made a mess out of it. 'They let off steam and the hate that had been burning in them.
During their years of slavery, they were forced to work like horses and had to live like pigs.
What a life those poor people must have led.
I sleep upstairs on the third floor.' And I just wanted you to know that this is the sort of thing I did frequently thereafter.
We requisitioned German homes, sometimes when people were still living there.
I went to the American occupation forces.
I got a list of names of the biggest Nazis in town, then I went to their house.
If they still lived there, I told them to evacuate.
I gave them, usually, about half a day to get out.
They screamed.
They hollered.
They didn't want to do it, but they better do it.
And sometimes, they already fled.
Like in this house in Naishtat, they fled their homes.
And it was-- it gave me great satisfaction to, to be able to chase these people out, especially when I knew their background.
There were lots of them.
But one-- since you asked that question, the one sticks in my mind was in the town of Goppingen in Germany where I received a list of houses that belonged to prominent Nazis.
Yes.
Not too far-- well, maybe 100 kilometers from Stuttgart, but it was in southern Germany.
The first day in, a colonel called me in and said, get some decent housing for us.
So I got the list from the occupation authorities, were Americans, who always had terrific intelligence, who knew who the big shots were, who knew where the Nazis were.
And they gave me this list.
And I drove up to a magnificent villa.
And there was a flag of Argentina hanging outside.
And this was a major Nazi in town, but they had this flag hanging out there.
So I knew that that was all phony baloney.
And I went to open the gates, went into the house, and this was a beautiful home, a piano in the living room, one of these circular stairwells leading to the second floor.
And as I entered the house-- or just prior to that, I had heard from home that my grandmother had been deported and most likely was among those people who had not survived the camps.
I heard that when I was a soldier in Germany.
She had been deported in 1942.
She was deported to-- she had moved to Holland and was deported from Holland either in '42 or '43-- I'm not sure when-- to a camp.
And I had gotten a letter from home saying that she more than likely, because of her age-- already she was 84-- had not survived.
I entered this home, and there came an elegant German woman about my grandmother's age.
She came down this winding stairwell, and she looked dressed in black, very dignified, like my grandmother used to dress.
And I see this woman come down.
And she says in a very arrogant German, [GERMAN]?
And I told her-- What you want?
What do want?
What you want?

And I told her that they have until 5 o'clock that evening to vacate the house, and not to take anything but blankets so they can sleep somewhere-- and I didn't care where-- and leave everything absolutely intact, and vacate the house.
Well, she was so indignant.
And I told her, I said, in German, I said that my grandmother looked very much like you.
And you know what your son-- I think it was, son who occupied the home-- did to my grandmother?
And I told her what I had found out, that she had been deported to a camp and was more than likely killed.
And she, of course, said she didn't know anything about this.
And she had no knowledge of it.
And her son certainly didn't do it.
Her son, by the way, was an SS obersturmfuhrer-- so had a very big rank in the SS.
And he also owned the major-- I think it was a steel mill in town.
He was not there.
He was either captured or-- he was just not there.
He also knew not to come home because we were looking for him, one of the top Nazis in town.
And she kept on protesting the fact that she had to be out at 5 o'clock.
I said, you know something?
I've changed my mind.
I said, I don't want you out at 5 o'clock, I want you out at 2 o'clock.
You will be out of this house at 2 o'clock, or else.
Well, when I came back at 2 o'clock, they were loading up a truck full of some mattresses and bedding, and they got out.
And I got some of the dirtiest looks that I ever got in my life, but I didn't care.
I want you to know.
These are the sort of stories I-- but the tape gets too long.
I don't want to talk about it, OK?
Yeah.
Had you heard at all about any of the concentration camps?
Yes.
In France already we were beginning to hear about the camps in the east, particularly-- you know, Germany was still occupied Germany.
And we didn't hear directly what camps in Germany.
We heard about the camps in the east.
Yes, we certainly did.
And then, as we were towards the end of the war, we saw some of these prisoners, ex-prisoners, come into southern Germany, those that were survivors.
They looked awful.
So yes, we heard about it then.
Just going back for one moment to this-- to Naishtat.
There was a slave labor camp there.
Yes, I believe there was a slave labor camp.
There were quite a few slave laborers that had been released by our troops when we got in.
They opened the gates, so to speak, to the camp.
Yeah.
And so this is where those, those Russians came from.
Yeah, that I just read the letter.
They came from these camps surrounding or in the city itself.
There were quite a number of what we call DPs in those days, all in that vicinity.
They were being used as slave laborers.
They were not Jewish people, these were Russians, and Polish, and from all Yugoslav, also from the captured nations, all different people.
Oh, yes.
Oh, absolutely.
Oh, yeah.
Dachau was probably the most famous camp, because it was among the very first ones in Germany itself.
Yeah.
Not to my knowledge, no.
At first, in France, to the Third Army.
And when we got to southern Germany, we were-- the Third Army was General Patton's army.
And then, from there, we went into southern Germany with the 7th Army.
Commanding general was General Patch.
Oh, yes.
Always with combat units until the war was over.
And then we were just by ourself until we were sent home again.
We were in a field south of Munich, when word came about that Dachau had been liberated.
And I had the most gruesome experience of my life.
And I would like, rather than tell you about it, read a letter which I wrote to my wife, which is rather explicit and tells you about my experience.
The letter is dated-- this is the original letter.
I keep it because I think it has sort of historic meaning.
It does to me, anyway.
This is the original letter, as you can see.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it is dated May the 3rd, 1945 with a news clipping.
The news clipping describes the capture of the Dachau concentration camp.
I believe I cut this out of the-- there was an American Army newspaper called And I only included this clipping.
And this is the letter. 'My dearest honey.
What I saw yesterday is really too horrible to write about, but I will make an attempt to recall all of the horrors I saw and hope that you can take it.
And this is not a sugar report.
I had an opportunity to visit the notorious concentration camp at Dachau along with our chaplain.' They had called that they needed chaplains and doctors, I believe.
And we were close by, so our chaplain then requisition a Jeep.
And I went with him as his interpreter, again, to the camp. 'You can see by enclosed report that we liberated the camp and its inmates just a few days ago.
And I am glad that I was able to see all of its horrors right after we killed the last SS man in charge.
And here is what I saw.

First of all, there were many thousands of prisoners, thousands of Jews--' I call them Pollacks, which is not a nice word-- 'Russians, Hungarians, French men, thousands of German political prisoners, and even women, behind barbed wire fences.
We can't let them out yet.
Too many are diseased and likeable to spread an epidemic.
So we feed the people a good diet now.
And an evacuation hospital furnishes medical attention, something they have never had before.
They were beaten and underfed, living on one turnip and a loaf of bread for eight men per day.
Well, those were the ones alive, anyway.
But so many were dead, killed, starved that it would take more than a day to count all the bodies I alone saw.
There were so many that it will take a few more days to cremate or bury all the bodies.
The first thing that struck us were a long row of box cars.
These were filled with dead human beings who had been shipped down to Dachau from Buchenwald and other camps.
They were kept in these closed box cars, and many died there of suffocation, no air, no ventilation, no food, no latrines.
Some tried to get out of those dead boxes and were shot by the SS guards as soon as they got off.
Their bodies were laying outside on the rails.
The next thing we saw was even more horrible.
It was a single story house with a huge backyard and huge dog houses for the most vicious types of dogs.

And here is a blueprint of it all.' And I drew a little picture there of, actually, how I found the buildings at the camp. 'As you can see, it was a house of murder where human beings were pushed alive into one entrance and came out dead at the other.
Some bodies were burned-- those in room number one.
There were three big furnaces in room number two filled with human bones.
And in room number three were supposed to-- those that were supposed to have been buried, 500 into one common grave.
There wasn't enough coal to burn all the people they killed.
Anyway, they never got around to burn or bury them all before we came because our troops got there before the SS expected us.
Both rooms are still filled high with gassed, shot, hanged, and tortured dead bodies, a sight I shall never forget.
Most all bodies in the room number three were dressed in prison clothing, striped suits.
Yet, but all bodies in room number one were naked.
Oh darling, it was a horrible sight.
The gas chamber was a room with airtight steel doors labeled, shower bath.' [NON-ENGLISH], what do they call it?
Whatever. 'They were regular shower heads built into the ceiling, but poison gas came out of these instead of water.
There were thousands of suits taken from the dead piled in the back yard.
And the Yugoslav slave I talked to said that he alone had been forced to burn more than 2,500 persons, he alone, during the last months.
They never worked more than a month or two in the furnace rooms.
And then, they were either killed themselves or sent to a different concentration camp, after a month of 24 hours a day 'steady work' quote, unquote.
The backyard had places where they shot people with dried blood still on the ground and a huge hole filled with remnants of human bones taken out of the furnace.
The whole affair had a high stone wall with an electric fence around it.
I shall never forget that yard.
And what I witnessed there as long-- will be with me as long as I live.' And it is, believe me. 'Some of the dogs had been taken away, others had been shot.
Like I said, we caught the guards by surprise.
And now, there are dead SS men laying everywhere around the camp, killed either by our troops or some of the inmates who got a hold of their guns.
Justice well done.
I only hope that none got away.
While we entered, General Patch and his staff as well as congressman and newspaper reporters started to look around everywhere.
I hope they can write what they saw and tell people at home about it.
I want you to tell all the people you know about this and read the letter I'll write to them to my folks, but not so detailed.

I also visited Munchen and entered remnants of Adolph Hitler's [GERMAN], his old hangout, and looked around town.' And I write about the [GERMAN] and what I saw there, which is anti-climatic, of course, after I read to you my letter about the camp.
And I say goodbye to my wife in the letter.
And I think that this letter tells it better than even my memory.
Even so, my memory is pretty good about all this.
Yes.
I walked up to the fence.
And the first thing I wanted to do-- I had filled our Jeep with food.
Hearing about the undernourishment, having seen some of the escaped and released prisoners, I had put food in the Jeep.

And the moment an American officer saw me walk up to the fence and started to talk to these guys, and he said, whatever you do, don't you dare give them any food because they get deadly sick from normal food.
They were just-- their, their stomachs wouldn't accept it.
And they had typhoid.
And they were diseased.
They were so sick.
They looked so terrible, some of them were just-- they were more dead than alive.
And then some of them died, you know?
